enrichment.short_description: Datree was a policy-enforcement and configuration-validation tool that scanned Kubernetes manifests and Infrastructure-as-Code files for misconfigurations before they reached production. It shipped as a CLI plus a hosted policy dashboard used in CI/CD pipelines and pre-commit hooks. The commercial company behind Datree closed in July 2023, and its GitHub repository was archived as read-only in June 2024, so the product is no longer actively developed, supported, or sold.
enrichment.pricing_model: Discontinued as a commercial SaaS product as of 2023; the remaining CLI codebase is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license
enrichment.starting_price: Not applicable, the commercial product has been discontinued and the open-source CLI is free to run in offline mode
enrichment.company_size: Company closed in July 2023 after operating with an estimated 11-50 employees at its peak
enrichment.headquarters: Tel Aviv, Israel (historical, prior to the company closing)
enrichment.seo_title: Datree Review 2026: What Happened and Current Status
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Datree was a Kubernetes and IaC policy-enforcement tool. See what it did, why the company closed in 2023, and where to look for active alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Datree was a policy-enforcement and configuration-validation platform built specifically for Kubernetes manifests and Infrastructure-as-Code files such as Helm charts, Kustomize configurations, and Terraform. Founded in 2017 by Shimon Tolts and Eyar Zilberman and headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, the company raised about 11 million US dollars across a seed round from TLV Partners in 2018 and an 8 million dollar Series A led with participation from Blumberg Capital in 2020. The core product was an open-source command-line interface, distributed on GitHub under the datreeio organization, that developers ran locally or inside CI/CD pipelines to catch Kubernetes misconfigurations, such as missing resource limits, disallowed image tags, or violations of best-practice rules, before manifests were applied to a cluster. Datree paired the CLI with a hosted web dashboard where teams could author, share, and centrally enforce custom policies, view historical validation results across repositories, and integrate checks into GitHub, GitLab, Jenkins, and other pipelines. It positioned itself as an end-to-end, code-to-cloud guardrail for platform and DevOps engineering teams who needed to prevent bad Kubernetes configurations from ever reaching production, complementing runtime security tools rather than replacing them. Datree integrated with policy-as-code engines and shipped a built-in library of best-practice rules covering workload reliability, security posture, and Kubernetes API conventions, and it supported writing custom rules in YAML or via Open Policy Agent. As of 2026, Datree is no longer an active commercial product. The company that built and sold it shut down in July 2023 after roughly six years in business without reaching sustainable profitability or being acquired; despite being informally linked in some third-party listings to acquirers such as Spectral or Check Point, no verifiable evidence confirms an acquisition of Datree itself, and the company's own GitHub documentation describes the closure as a shutdown rather than a sale. The GitHub repository datreeio/datree was subsequently archived by its owner on June 6, 2024, meaning it is now read-only and accepts no further code changes, bug fixes, or security patches. The hosted dashboard, centralized policy registry, and related cloud services tied to the commercial offering are no longer available. Existing users are directed to run the Datree CLI as a standalone, offline tool with a reduced feature set, relying on locally stored policies rather than the cloud dashboard. The underlying codebase remains publicly accessible under the Apache 2.0 open-source license, so technically minded teams can still fork it, self-host validation logic, or extract individual policy rules, but there is no vendor support, roadmap, or security patching going forward. For a directory or comparison context in 2026, Datree should be described accurately as a discontinued commercial product and an archived, unmaintained open-source project, useful mainly as a historical reference point when evaluating currently active Kubernetes policy tools such as Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper, Conftest, or Checkov, all of which continue to receive active development and community support.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Datree Did
paragraphs: Datree provided automated policy checks for Kubernetes YAML manifests and related Infrastructure-as-Code files, catching problems like missing health checks, overly permissive security contexts, or non-compliant naming conventions before deployment.

The tool combined a free, open-source CLI with an optional hosted dashboard that let teams write and enforce custom policies across repositories and pipelines, effectively acting as a linter and guardrail layer between a developer's local machine and a production Kubernetes cluster.

long_form_blocks.heading: Why Datree Is No Longer Active
paragraphs: Datree's parent company ceased commercial operations in July 2023 after failing to reach sustainable profitability, and no acquisition of the business has been publicly confirmed despite some third-party company databases loosely associating it with other Israeli security vendors.

The project's GitHub repository was formally archived on June 6, 2024, closing it to new commits, issues, and security fixes. This means teams still relying on Datree in production pipelines are running unmaintained software and should plan a migration.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Teams Use Instead
paragraphs: Teams that previously used Datree for Kubernetes policy enforcement have generally migrated to actively maintained tools such as Kyverno, Open Policy Agent with Gatekeeper, Conftest, or Checkov, which cover similar use cases with ongoing community and vendor support.

Because Datree's dashboard and centralized policy registry are gone, any residual use of the Datree CLI today should be treated as a purely local, offline linting step rather than a full policy-enforcement platform.

features.title: Kubernetes manifest validation
features.description: Scanned YAML manifests against built-in and custom rules covering reliability, security, and Kubernetes API best practices.

features.title: CI/CD pipeline integration
features.description: Ran as a step in GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, and other pipelines to block non-compliant manifests before merge or deploy.

features.title: Custom policy authoring
features.description: Let teams define their own rules in YAML or via Open Policy Agent to reflect organization-specific standards.

features.title: Centralized policy dashboard
features.description: Offered a hosted web interface for managing policies and viewing validation history across repositories, now discontinued.

features.title: Pre-commit and local CLI checks
features.description: Allowed developers to validate manifests on their own machines before ever pushing code.

features.title: Schema validation
features.description: Checked manifests against official and custom Kubernetes JSON schemas to catch structural errors.

features.title: Rule library
features.description: Shipped with a curated set of best-practice rules that could be enabled or disabled per team.

features.title: Open-source CLI
features.description: Released the core scanning engine under the Apache 2.0 license, still downloadable and runnable in offline mode.

pros: Was straightforward to add as a single step in existing CI/CD pipelines
Covered a broad, curated library of Kubernetes best-practice rules out of the box
Supported custom policies for teams with organization-specific standards
Free, open-source CLI remains downloadable and can still run basic offline checks
Clear, human-readable violation output that made it easy to see exactly what to fix
cons: The company has shut down and the product receives no further updates or security patches
The GitHub repository is archived and closed to new contributions as of June 2024
The hosted dashboard, centralized policy registry, and cloud sync are no longer available
Teams relying on it today are running unsupported software with no vendor accountability
No clear migration tooling was provided to move historical policies to another platform
Rule coverage has not evolved with newer Kubernetes API versions since the shutdown

faqs.question: Is Datree still available in 2026
faqs.answer: The open-source CLI code is still publicly downloadable on GitHub, but the commercial product, hosted dashboard, and company behind it are no longer operating. The repository was archived as read-only in June 2024.

faqs.question: Was Datree acquired by Spectral or Check Point
faqs.answer: No verifiable evidence confirms this. Check Point acquired Spectral, a separate Israeli developer-security startup, in 2022, but Datree's own documentation describes its July 2023 closure as a shutdown, not an acquisition.

faqs.question: Can I still use the Datree CLI today
faqs.answer: Yes, the CLI can still be run locally in offline mode for basic Kubernetes manifest checks, but without the cloud dashboard, centralized policy registry, or vendor support that the commercial product once provided.

faqs.question: What did Datree cost when it was active
faqs.answer: Datree offered a free-forever CLI tier alongside paid team and enterprise plans with centralized policy management, though exact historical pricing is no longer published since the product was discontinued.

faqs.question: What should teams use instead of Datree
faqs.answer: Actively maintained alternatives for Kubernetes policy enforcement include Kyverno, Open Policy Agent with Gatekeeper, Conftest, and Checkov.

faqs.question: Who founded Datree
faqs.answer: Datree was founded in 2017 by Shimon Tolts and Eyar Zilberman and was headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel.

faqs.question: Does Datree still receive security patches
faqs.answer: No. Because the repository is archived, it does not receive security patches, bug fixes, or new Kubernetes API compatibility updates.

faqs.question: Is there a risk to using Datree in production pipelines now
faqs.answer: Yes, since the tool is unmaintained, teams should treat it as a legacy stopgap at most and plan a migration to an actively supported policy-enforcement tool.

use_cases.title: Historical reference for Kubernetes policy tooling
use_cases.description: Useful as a case study of an early Kubernetes policy-enforcement product when researching the evolution of the category.
use_cases.audience: DevOps and platform engineering teams researching tooling history

use_cases.title: Offline manifest linting
use_cases.description: The archived open-source CLI can still be run locally as a basic, no-frills Kubernetes manifest linter.
use_cases.audience: Individual developers wanting a quick, free local check

use_cases.title: Migration planning
use_cases.description: Teams still running the legacy Datree CLI can use this profile to understand why it is time to migrate to an actively maintained alternative.
use_cases.audience: Engineering leads auditing unmaintained dependencies in CI/CD pipelines

enrichment.short_description: Datrium was a hybrid cloud data platform and converged infrastructure company known for its DVX system, which paired on-premises compute nodes with cloud-based data management and built-in disaster recovery. VMware acquired Datrium in 2020 for its disaster-recovery technology, then discontinued the DVX hardware and hyperconverged software line within weeks of closing the deal. Datrium's disaster-recovery-as-a-service technology lives on today as VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery, now sold under Broadcom-owned VMware.
enrichment.pricing_model: Discontinued as a standalone company and product line; its surviving DRaaS technology is sold as VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery under VMware or Broadcom subscription pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Not applicable, Datrium as a standalone product no longer exists
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed after being absorbed into VMware in 2020; reported to have roughly a few hundred employees at the time of acquisition
enrichment.headquarters: Sunnyvale, California, United States (historical, prior to the 2020 VMware acquisition)
enrichment.seo_title: Datrium Review 2026: History, VMware Acquisition and Status
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Datrium built hybrid cloud converged infrastructure before VMware acquired it in 2020. Learn what happened to Datrium and where its technology lives today.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Datrium was a hybrid cloud data platform and converged infrastructure company founded in 2012 and headquartered in Sunnyvale, California. Its flagship product, the DVX system, combined commodity on-premises compute nodes running a hyperconverged software stack with a cloud-connected data management layer, aiming to let enterprises run virtual machines on-premises while keeping backup, disaster recovery, and long-term data retention in the cloud without needing separate backup appliances or software. Datrium marketed DVX as a way to collapse primary storage, backup, and disaster recovery into a single platform, and it later expanded into a pure disaster-recovery-as-a-service offering aimed specifically at protecting VMware vSphere workloads by replicating them to the cloud. On July 1, 2020, VMware announced its intent to acquire Datrium, and the deal closed later that year for a total purchase price, net of cash acquired, of about 137 million US dollars, made up primarily of roughly 25 million dollars in identifiable intangible assets and about 91 million dollars in goodwill. VMware's stated rationale was to strengthen its VMware Site Recovery and broader disaster-recovery-as-a-service portfolio by adding a cost-optimized option for protecting data stored either on-premises or on Amazon Web Services infrastructure. Notably, VMware moved quickly after closing the acquisition: in August 2020, just over a month later, VMware discontinued the Datrium DVX hyperconverged hardware and software line effective immediately, stopping new hardware orders from new customers, allowing existing customers to place additional orders only through October 31, 2020, and ultimately winding down support for existing DVX deployments by 2023. Rather than continuing to sell Datrium's on-premises hyperconverged infrastructure, VMware chose to keep and rebrand only the disaster-recovery-as-a-service component of Datrium's technology. That surviving piece was relaunched as VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery, a cloud-native DRaaS offering that, unlike the original Datrium DRaaS, no longer requires Datrium DVX hardware on-premises and instead works with any storage supported by VMware vSphere. As of 2026, Datrium does not exist as an independent company, brand, or purchasable product; VMware itself was subsequently acquired by Broadcom in November 2023, meaning the technology lineage that started at Datrium now sits several ownership layers deep inside Broadcom's VMware Cloud Foundation and disaster-recovery portfolio. For anyone researching Datrium today, whether for a directory listing, a competitive analysis, or historical due diligence, the accurate framing is that Datrium was a converged infrastructure and hybrid cloud vendor whose hardware line was fully discontinued in 2020 and whose remaining intellectual property is embedded in VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery, a Broadcom-owned product, rather than being available as a standalone Datrium offering.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Datrium Built
paragraphs: Datrium's core product, DVX, was a converged infrastructure system that split compute and data management between on-premises hardware and a cloud-connected control plane, letting enterprises run VMware virtual machines locally while pushing backup and disaster-recovery data to the cloud.

The company later built a standalone disaster-recovery-as-a-service product aimed at protecting VMware vSphere environments by replicating workloads off-premises without requiring a full parallel data center.

long_form_blocks.heading: The VMware Acquisition and Product Shutdown
paragraphs: VMware announced its intent to acquire Datrium on July 1, 2020, and closed the deal later that year for approximately 137 million US dollars net of cash acquired, primarily to bolster its own disaster-recovery-as-a-service portfolio.

Barely a month after closing, VMware discontinued the Datrium DVX hyperconverged hardware and software line, stopped taking new hardware orders, and wound down support for existing deployments over the following years, effectively ending Datrium as an independent product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Where Datrium's Technology Lives Today
paragraphs: Only Datrium's disaster-recovery-as-a-service technology survived the acquisition, relaunched under the name VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery, which works with standard VMware vSphere-supported storage rather than requiring proprietary Datrium hardware.

Following Broadcom's acquisition of VMware in November 2023, that surviving technology now sits within Broadcom's VMware Cloud Foundation and disaster-recovery product lines rather than being sold under the Datrium name.

features.title: Converged compute and cloud data management
features.description: DVX combined on-premises compute nodes with a cloud-connected data management layer for primary storage, backup, and disaster recovery in one system.

features.title: Built-in backup and disaster recovery
features.description: Eliminated the need for separate backup appliances by handling data protection natively within the platform.

features.title: VMware vSphere integration
features.description: Was purpose-built to protect and replicate VMware virtual machine workloads.

features.title: Cloud-based long-term retention
features.description: Pushed older backup data to the cloud for cost-effective long-term storage rather than keeping it on local hardware.

features.title: Disaster-recovery-as-a-service
features.description: Offered a pure DRaaS product that replicated on-premises workloads to the cloud without requiring a secondary physical data center.

features.title: Hyperconverged hardware appliances
features.description: Sold purpose-built DVX hardware appliances, discontinued by VMware in August 2020.

pros: Pioneered a genuinely novel architecture that split compute and data management between on-premises and cloud
Simplified disaster recovery for VMware-heavy environments by removing the need for a full secondary data center
Its surviving DRaaS technology continues to be developed and supported today as VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery
Was tightly integrated with VMware vSphere, which many enterprises already ran
Received meaningful venture backing and industry attention prior to its acquisition
cons: The Datrium brand and DVX hardware line no longer exist; VMware discontinued them in August 2020
Existing DVX customers had a hard cutoff for ordering new hardware and a defined end of support
The company is not independently operable or purchasable today under the Datrium name
Its surviving technology is now several ownership layers removed, sitting inside Broadcom-owned VMware
Historical Datrium documentation and support resources are largely superseded by VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery materials
Buyers researching Datrium in 2026 cannot get a current quote, demo, or support contract under that name

faqs.question: Does Datrium still exist as a company in 2026
faqs.answer: No. Datrium was acquired by VMware in 2020 and no longer operates as an independent company or brand.

faqs.question: Can I still buy Datrium DVX hardware
faqs.answer: No. VMware discontinued the DVX hyperconverged hardware and software line effective immediately in August 2020, about a month after closing the acquisition.

faqs.question: What happened to Datrium's disaster recovery product
faqs.answer: Its disaster-recovery-as-a-service technology was relaunched under the name VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery, which now works with any VMware vSphere-supported storage rather than requiring Datrium hardware.

faqs.question: How much did VMware pay to acquire Datrium
faqs.answer: VMware's purchase price, net of cash acquired, was approximately 137 million US dollars, consisting mainly of about 25 million dollars in identifiable intangible assets and roughly 91 million dollars in goodwill.

faqs.question: Who owns Datrium's technology now
faqs.answer: VMware itself was acquired by Broadcom in November 2023, so the surviving Datrium-derived disaster-recovery technology now sits within Broadcom's VMware product portfolio.

faqs.question: When was Datrium founded and where was it based
faqs.answer: Datrium was founded in 2012 and was headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, prior to its 2020 acquisition by VMware.

faqs.question: Is there support for legacy Datrium DVX deployments
faqs.answer: VMware wound down support for existing DVX deployments by 2023, so legacy Datrium hardware in production today is effectively unsupported.

faqs.question: What should I evaluate instead of Datrium in 2026
faqs.answer: Buyers researching hybrid cloud disaster recovery today should evaluate VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery directly, along with other current DRaaS vendors, rather than looking for Datrium as a standalone purchase.

use_cases.title: Historical and competitive research
use_cases.description: Useful for understanding the lineage of VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery and how converged infrastructure vendors have consolidated into larger platforms.
use_cases.audience: Analysts and IT researchers studying the hyperconverged infrastructure market

use_cases.title: Legacy deployment audits
use_cases.description: Organizations still running old Datrium DVX hardware can use this profile to understand why support has ended and plan a migration.
use_cases.audience: IT operations teams managing aging on-premises infrastructure

use_cases.title: Due diligence on VMware's disaster recovery lineage
use_cases.description: Helps buyers evaluating VMware Cloud Disaster Recovery understand the underlying technology's origin at Datrium.

enrichment.short_description: DB Designer, at dbdesigner.net, is an online database schema design and entity-relationship diagramming tool that lets developers and data teams build database models visually without writing SQL by hand. It supports forward engineering to SQL for major databases and reverse engineering from existing MySQL, PostgreSQL, and Oracle databases. The tool offers a free tier plus paid monthly plans for larger model limits, along with free access for students, educators, non-profits, and open-source contributors.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription with a free tier and paid monthly plans based on the number of database models and tables
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available; paid plans start at 9 US dollars per month for the Basic plan
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed, operates as a small, independently run software product
enrichment.seo_title: DB Designer Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DB Designer is an online ER diagram and database schema design tool. See its pricing, features, forward and reverse engineering support, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: DB Designer, hosted at dbdesigner.net, is a cloud-based database design and entity-relationship modeling tool that has been helping teams design database schemas online since 2006. It lets users build a visual database model much like creating a UML diagram, either starting from a blank canvas, choosing from ready-made templates, or reverse engineering an existing database, and it is compatible with MySQL, MS SQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQLite for forward engineering, while reverse engineering is currently supported for MySQL, PostgreSQL, and Oracle specifically. Once a model is complete, DB Designer can forward-engineer it into a one-click SQL script for the target database engine, which is the core workflow the product is built around: designing visually first, then generating accurate, ready-to-run SQL rather than hand-writing CREATE TABLE statements. The platform is aimed at developers, database administrators, students, and teams who want a faster and less error-prone way to plan schemas before implementation, and it emphasizes collaboration, letting cloud-stored designs be shared with teammates either for editing or view-only access by entering their email addresses; team members with access can also leave live comments directly on the diagram, which speeds up schema review cycles. Additional workflow features include autocomplete for data types, automatic layout sorting, live validation of the model as it is built, keyboard shortcuts for faster editing, instant auto-save, a full revision history with one-click restore to any prior version, and the ability to clone or duplicate models and export them as images or PDFs for documentation and stakeholder review. Pricing follows a freemium structure. The free Starter plan supports up to two database models with a maximum of ten tables per model, which is enough for small projects, prototypes, or evaluating the tool. The paid Basic plan, at 9 US dollars per month, raises the limit to five database models with twenty-five tables per model, while the Unlimited plan, at 17 US dollars per month, removes model and table limits entirely for teams working on larger or more numerous schemas. DB Designer also offers three free specialty plans with unlimited models and tables: an Academic plan for current students and educators, a Non-Profit plan for qualifying non-profit organizations, and an Open Source plan for contributors to open-source projects, all of which otherwise carry the same feature set as the paid Unlimited tier. Team collaboration features are available across all plan levels rather than being gated to higher tiers, and the company states there are no hidden fees or long-term contracts, with users able to cancel, upgrade, or downgrade at any time and retain their data through plan changes. As a directory entry in 2026, DB Designer is best understood as a lightweight, browser-based alternative to heavier desktop ER modeling tools, well suited to teams that want quick visual schema design, straightforward SQL generation, and low-friction collaboration without installing dedicated database modeling software.

long_form_blocks.heading: How DB Designer Works
paragraphs: DB Designer centers on a visual, UML-style canvas where users build entity-relationship diagrams representing tables, columns, data types, and relationships, either from scratch, from a template, or by reverse engineering an existing MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Oracle database.

Once a model is finished, the tool forward-engineers it into a one-click SQL script targeted at MySQL, MS SQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, or SQLite, letting teams design first and generate implementation-ready SQL rather than writing schema definitions by hand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Collaboration and Version History
paragraphs: Designs are stored in the cloud and can be shared with teammates for either editing or view-only access, with live commenting available once collaborators are added by email, which supports asynchronous schema review.
Every change is auto-saved, and a full revision history lets teams roll back to any prior version with one click, reducing the risk of losing work during iterative schema design.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Tiers and Free Access Programs
paragraphs: The free Starter plan caps users at two models and ten tables per model, while the paid Basic plan (9 US dollars per month) raises that to five models and twenty-five tables, and the Unlimited plan (17 US dollars per month) removes those caps entirely.

Students, educators, qualifying non-profit organizations, and open-source contributors can apply for free plans that match the Unlimited tier's model and table limits, extending full functionality to those groups at no cost.

features.title: Visual ER diagram builder
features.description: Design database schemas on a drag-and-drop canvas similar to building a UML diagram, without writing raw SQL.

features.title: Forward engineering to SQL
features.description: Generate a ready-to-run SQL script for MySQL, MS SQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, or SQLite in one click from a finished model.

features.title: Reverse engineering
features.description: Import an existing MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Oracle database and automatically generate its diagram.

features.title: Team collaboration and sharing
features.description: Share cloud-stored models with teammates for editing or viewing, with live commenting on the diagram itself.

features.title: Templates and autocomplete
features.description: Start from ready-made templates and use data-type autocomplete and automatic sorting to speed up modeling.

features.title: Revision history and one-click restore
features.description: Every change is versioned, so teams can roll back to any earlier state of a model instantly.

features.title: Cloning and duplication
features.description: Duplicate an existing model or table structure to quickly start variations of a schema.

features.title: Image and PDF export
features.description: Export finished diagrams as images or PDFs for documentation, presentations, or stakeholder review.

pros: Free tier is genuinely usable for small projects with two models and ten tables per model
Generates production-ready SQL for several major database engines in one click
Runs entirely in the browser with no software installation required
Free plans for students, educators, non-profits, and open-source contributors extend full functionality at no cost
Team collaboration and live commenting are included on every plan, not gated to higher tiers
Auto-save plus full revision history reduces the risk of losing design work
cons: Reverse engineering is limited to MySQL, PostgreSQL, and Oracle, excluding MS SQL and SQLite
Free and Basic plans have relatively low model and table caps compared to some competitors
Company details such as headquarters and team size are not publicly disclosed, which may concern enterprise buyers doing vendor due diligence
As a browser-based tool, it depends on a stable internet connection for editing and saving
Does not appear to offer an on-premises or self-hosted deployment option for teams with strict data residency requirements
Advanced database-specific features supported by dedicated desktop modeling tools may not all be available

faqs.question: Is DB Designer free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, DB Designer has a free Starter plan supporting up to two database models with ten tables per model, and free specialty plans for students, educators, non-profits, and open-source contributors.

faqs.question: How much does DB Designer cost
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 9 US dollars per month for the Basic plan (five models, twenty-five tables per model), with an Unlimited plan at 17 US dollars per month for unlimited models and tables.

faqs.question: Which databases does DB Designer support
faqs.answer: It can forward-engineer SQL for MySQL, MS SQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQLite, and it supports reverse engineering from MySQL, PostgreSQL, and Oracle.

faqs.question: Can I collaborate with a team in DB Designer
faqs.answer: Yes, cloud-stored models can be shared with teammates by email for editing or viewing, and collaborators can leave live comments directly on the diagram.

faqs.question: Does DB Designer offer a free plan for students
faqs.answer: Yes, current students and educators can apply for a free Academic plan with unlimited models and tables.

faqs.question: Can I export my database diagrams
faqs.answer: Yes, models can be exported as images or PDFs for documentation or presentations.

faqs.question: Does DB Designer support version history
faqs.answer: Yes, changes are auto-saved and a full revision history allows one-click restoration of any prior version.

faqs.question: Is there a contract or lock-in with paid plans
faqs.answer: No, the company states plans can be cancelled, upgraded, or downgraded at any time with no hidden fees or long-term contracts.

use_cases.title: Prototyping a new application's database schema
use_cases.description: Developers can visually plan tables and relationships before writing any SQL, then generate implementation-ready scripts.
use_cases.audience: Backend developers and solo founders building new applications

use_cases.title: Documenting an existing production database
use_cases.description: Teams can reverse engineer a live MySQL, PostgreSQL, or Oracle database into a visual diagram for onboarding or audits.

use_cases.title: Classroom and coursework database design
use_cases.description: Students and educators can use the free Academic plan to design and iterate on schemas for coursework without cost.

use_cases.title: Cross-functional schema review
use_cases.description: Product and engineering teams can share a model for view-only feedback and comments before implementation.

features: For current students and educators only
For contributors of open-source projects only

enrichment.short_description: DB-IP is an IP geolocation database and API service operated by Eris Networks, providing location, network, and threat-intelligence data tied to IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. It has offered geolocation services since 2010 and covers the entire IPv4 and IPv6 address space with more than 45 million address blocks across more than 215,000 cities worldwide. The company sells tiered API subscriptions plus downloadable offline databases, and offers a free Lite database tier for lower-accuracy, non-commercial use.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered monthly API subscriptions plus one-time or subscription downloadable database licenses, with a free Lite database tier
enrichment.starting_price: Approximately 8.29 euros per month for the Starter Basic API plan
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed, operates as a small, focused data provider
enrichment.seo_title: DB-IP Review 2026: IP Geolocation Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DB-IP offers IP geolocation and network intelligence data via API and downloadable databases. Compare its pricing, accuracy, and features for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: DB-IP is an IP geolocation and network intelligence data provider operated by Eris Networks S.A.S, headquartered in Perros-Guirec, France, that has offered geolocation services since 2010. Its core product maps IP addresses to real-world location and network attributes, covering the entire IPv4 and IPv6 address space with a database of more than 45 million address blocks spanning over 2 million unique locations across more than 215,000 cities worldwide. DB-IP reports accuracy above 97 percent at the city level and 99.99 percent at the country level globally, figures the company positions as competitive with larger geolocation vendors. Data is delivered in two main ways: a real-time API that developers query per request, and downloadable offline database files (in formats such as MMDB and CSV) that customers can integrate directly into their own infrastructure without making network calls, which is useful for high-volume or latency-sensitive applications. The API and database products are organized into three escalating data tiers. The Basic tier returns core location information such as country, region, city, GPS coordinates, district, zip code, time zone, and a Geonames unique place identifier. The Core tier includes everything in Basic and adds detailed network and connectivity data, including autonomous system (AS) number and name, ISP and organization name, and connection type. The Extended tier includes everything in Core and layers on security-relevant data such as proxy and crawler detection and threat-level assessment, aimed at fraud prevention, content localization, and access-control use cases. For the Basic API specifically, DB-IP publishes three subscription levels: a Starter plan around 8.29 euros per month covering roughly 2,000 to 50,000 requests per day with HTTPS access, localization in more than fifty languages, batch queries of up to 256 IPs, a single CORS domain, and email support; a Premium plan around 99.90 euros per month covering 200,000 to 1,000,000 requests per day with up to five CORS domains, a 99.95 percent uptime guarantee, and priority support; and an Enterprise plan around 499.90 euros per month offering unlimited daily requests, unlimited batch queries, unlimited CORS domains, a 99.99 percent uptime guarantee, dedicated infrastructure, and priority support. Core and Extended API tiers, along with downloadable database products, are priced separately and generally require checking DB-IP's site or contacting sales for current rates, since pricing for higher data tiers is not uniformly published in one place. Localization is a notable differentiator: DB-IP supports country, region, district, and city name localization in more than fifty languages for its API responses, while the downloadable MMDB-format databases include localized place names in a smaller set of major languages such as English, French, German, Spanish, Portuguese, Chinese, Japanese, Russian, Persian, and Korean. DB-IP also offers a free IP-to-City Lite database aimed at developers and non-commercial or lower-budget use cases who need basic city-level geolocation without a paid subscription, though free-tier accuracy and update frequency are reduced compared to paid commercial databases. As a directory entry in 2026, DB-IP is best understood as a mid-market IP geolocation and network intelligence provider competing with vendors like MaxMind and IPinfo, differentiated primarily by its extensive language localization support, tiered data depth (Basic, Core, Extended), and the flexibility of choosing between a live API and self-hosted offline database files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Data Coverage and Accuracy
paragraphs: DB-IP's database covers the entire IPv4 and IPv6 address space with more than 45 million address blocks across over 2 million unique locations in more than 215,000 cities worldwide, and the company reports accuracy above 97 percent at the city level and 99.99 percent at the country level.
Data is refreshed and delivered either through a real-time API or as downloadable offline database files, giving customers flexibility between live lookups and self-hosted, low-latency integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Basic, Core, and Extended Data Tiers
paragraphs: The Basic tier covers core location data such as country, city, GPS coordinates, and time zone. The Core tier adds ISP and autonomous system network data on top of Basic. The Extended tier layers on security signals like proxy, crawler, and threat-level detection.

This tiered structure lets customers pay only for the depth of data their use case requires, from simple content localization up to fraud-prevention and access-control scenarios that need network and threat intelligence.

long_form_blocks.heading: Basic API Pricing Breakdown
paragraphs: The Starter Basic API plan costs approximately 8.29 euros per month for 2,000 to 50,000 requests per day, the Premium plan costs approximately 99.90 euros per month for 200,000 to 1,000,000 requests per day, and the Enterprise plan costs approximately 499.90 euros per month for unlimited daily requests.
Higher plans add practical guarantees for production use, including higher uptime SLAs, more CORS domains, dedicated infrastructure at the Enterprise level, and priority support.

features.title: Full IPv4 and IPv6 coverage
features.description: Maps the entire IPv4 and IPv6 address space across more than 45 million address blocks.

features.title: City, region, and country geolocation
features.description: Returns granular location data including GPS coordinates, district, and zip code for supported addresses.

features.title: ISP and network intelligence
features.description: Core and Extended tiers add autonomous system number, ISP name, organization, and connection type.

features.title: Proxy, crawler, and threat detection
features.description: The Extended tier flags proxy usage, crawler traffic, and assigns a threat-level score for security and fraud use cases.

features.title: Multi-language localization
features.description: Supports place-name localization in more than fifty languages via the API and a smaller set of major languages in offline MMDB databases.

features.title: Real-time API and offline databases
features.description: Offers both a queryable REST API and downloadable database files for self-hosted, low-latency integrations.

features.title: Batch query support
features.description: Allows batch lookups of multiple IP addresses in a single request on paid plans.

features.title: Free Lite database
features.description: Provides a free, lower-accuracy IP-to-City Lite database for non-commercial or budget-constrained use.

pros: Covers the entire global IPv4 and IPv6 address space with reported high accuracy at country and city level
Offers both a live API and downloadable offline databases, giving integration flexibility
Supports localization in more than fifty languages, unusually broad for a geolocation provider
Tiered Basic, Core, and Extended data levels let customers pay only for the depth of data they need
Free Lite database option makes it accessible for small or non-commercial projects
Enterprise plan includes dedicated infrastructure and a 99.99 percent uptime guarantee
cons: Full pricing for Core, Extended, and offline database licenses is not uniformly published and often requires contacting sales
Company size and detailed corporate background are not publicly disclosed
Starter and Premium plan request caps may be limiting for rapidly scaling applications
IP geolocation data is inherently probabilistic and can be inaccurate for VPN, mobile, or corporate NAT traffic regardless of vendor
Offline MMDB localization covers fewer languages than the live API's fifty-plus languages
As a smaller, France-based provider, it has less brand recognition than larger competitors like MaxMind

faqs.question: What is DB-IP used for
faqs.answer: DB-IP is used to determine the geographic location, network provider, and security risk associated with an IP address, commonly for content localization, fraud prevention, analytics, and access control.

faqs.question: How much does DB-IP cost
faqs.answer: The Basic API Starter plan costs about 8.29 euros per month, Premium is about 99.90 euros per month, and Enterprise is about 499.90 euros per month, based on daily request volume.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of DB-IP
faqs.answer: Yes, DB-IP offers a free downloadable IP-to-City Lite database for non-commercial or lower-accuracy use cases.

faqs.question: How accurate is DB-IP's geolocation data
faqs.answer: The company reports accuracy above 97 percent at the city level and 99.99 percent at the country level worldwide.

faqs.question: Does DB-IP support IPv6
faqs.answer: Yes, DB-IP covers the entire IPv6 address space in addition to IPv4.

faqs.question: Can I download DB-IP's database instead of using the API
faqs.answer: Yes, DB-IP offers downloadable offline database files in addition to its real-time API, suitable for self-hosted, low-latency integrations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between DB-IP's Basic, Core, and Extended tiers
faqs.answer: Basic returns core location data, Core adds ISP and network data, and Extended adds proxy, crawler, and threat-level detection on top of Core.

faqs.question: Who operates DB-IP
faqs.answer: DB-IP is operated by Eris Networks S.A.S, headquartered in Perros-Guirec, France, and has offered geolocation services since 2010.

use_cases.title: Website content localization
use_cases.description: Automatically serve region-appropriate language, currency, or content based on a visitor's IP-derived location.

use_cases.title: Fraud and risk scoring
use_cases.description: Combine proxy detection and threat-level data from the Extended tier to flag suspicious sign-ups or transactions.
use_cases.audience: Trust and safety and fraud-prevention teams

use_cases.title: Analytics and audience insights
use_cases.description: Enrich web or app analytics with visitor city, region, and ISP data for reporting and segmentation.

use_cases.title: Access control and compliance
use_cases.description: Restrict or allow access to content or services based on country or region using geolocation lookups.

enrichment.short_description: DeaDBeeF is a free, open-source, plugin-based audio player originally built for Linux and other Unix-like systems, with additional builds for Windows and macOS. First released in 2009, it emphasizes a modular architecture where nearly every feature, from format decoders to on-screen notifications, is delivered through plugins that users can enable or disable. It supports a very wide range of audio formats and is popular among Linux users who want a lightweight, highly customizable music player.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source, no paid tiers
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable, DeaDBeeF is a community-maintained open-source project rather than a company
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable, distributed open-source project with no corporate headquarters
enrichment.seo_title: DeaDBeeF Review 2026: Features and How It Compares
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DeaDBeeF is a free, open-source, plugin-based audio player for Linux. See its supported formats, plugin system, and how it compares to other music players.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: DeaDBeeF is a free and open-source, plugin-based audio player first released in August 2009, primarily aimed at Linux and other Unix-like operating systems but also available on Windows and, less commonly, macOS. The name is a reference to the classic hexadecimal debug value 0xDEADBEEF, a nod to the project's developer-oriented, tinkerer-friendly design philosophy. The defining characteristic of DeaDBeeF is its modular plugin architecture: rather than shipping as a fixed, monolithic application, the player exposes almost all of its functionality, including format decoders, output backends, user-interface behavior, and on-screen notifications, as individual plugins that users can enable, disable, or replace, letting each installation be tailored precisely to what a given user wants without carrying unnecessary bloat. Because of this architecture, DeaDBeeF supports an unusually broad range of audio formats out of the box or via plugins, including MP3, OGG, FLAC and OGA, APE, WAV, WavPack, AAC and M4A, ALAC, Musepack, TTA, WMA, SHN, and tracker or chiptune formats such as SID, NSF, MOD, S3M, VTX, VGM and VGZ, PSF, and MIDI, plus audio CDs, and it can additionally decode any format supported by underlying libraries like ffmpeg, dumb, Game Music Emu, libsndfile, and adplug when those plugins are installed. Notable user-facing features include native CUE sheet handling for gapless playback of albums ripped as a single file, an on-screen-display notification plugin that pops up track information when a song changes, an album-art plugin capable of pulling artwork from multiple online and local sources, and the ability to play music directly from ZIP archives without extracting them first. The interface itself is customizable, and later versions, such as the 1.10.x release series, moved to GTK3 as the default toolkit while adding features like FLAC network streaming support, a lyrics plugin, and improved album-art display modes, reflecting ongoing incremental development by its maintainer and contributor community. As a genuinely community-driven open-source project rather than a commercial company, DeaDBeeF has no pricing, no company headquarters, and no formal company size; it is developed collaboratively, distributed under an open-source license, and made available through package managers on most major Linux distributions, as well as via direct downloads and unofficial PPAs for Ubuntu-based systems, and through SourceForge, where its official project page and downloads are hosted. It competes in the same general space as other lightweight, format-agnostic Linux audio players such as Audacious, Clementine, and Strawberry, but differentiates itself specifically through the depth of its plugin ecosystem and its especially wide native format support, including many niche tracker and chiptune formats rarely supported out of the box by mainstream music players. For a directory entry in 2026, DeaDBeeF should be positioned as a free, technically capable, and highly configurable audio player best suited to Linux users, audiophiles, and retro-format enthusiasts who value plugin-level customization over a polished, opinionated, all-in-one media player experience, and who are comfortable configuring plugins to get exactly the feature set they want rather than relying on a fixed built-in toolset.

long_form_blocks.heading: Plugin-Based Architecture
paragraphs: DeaDBeeF's defining feature is that nearly all of its functionality, from format decoding to notifications to album-art fetching, is delivered through individually enableable plugins rather than being built into a fixed core application.

This means users are not locked into a single feature set; they can strip the player down to a minimal, lightweight decoder and interface, or extend it with a wide range of optional plugins depending on what they need.

long_form_blocks.heading: Audio Format Support
paragraphs: DeaDBeeF natively supports a very wide range of formats including MP3, FLAC, OGG, APE, WAV, WavPack, AAC, ALAC, Musepack, TTA, WMA, and audio CDs, plus tracker and chiptune formats like SID, NSF, MOD, S3M, and VGM.

Through additional plugins built on libraries such as ffmpeg, Game Music Emu, and libsndfile, it can decode an even broader set of formats, making it a popular choice for users with large or unusually formatted music collections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Platforms and How to Get It
paragraphs: DeaDBeeF is primarily distributed for Linux and other Unix-like systems, with builds also available for Windows and macOS, and it is packaged in the repositories of many major Linux distributions as well as through community PPAs for Ubuntu.

As a free, open-source project hosted on SourceForge and elsewhere, it has no purchase process, license fees, or paid tiers; users simply download or install it through their distribution's package manager.

features.title: Modular plugin system
features.description: Nearly every feature, from decoders to UI behavior, is delivered as an independently enableable plugin.

features.title: Wide native format support
features.description: Plays MP3, FLAC, OGG, APE, WAV, AAC, ALAC, WMA, and many tracker and chiptune formats like SID, MOD, and NSF out of the box.

features.title: Gapless playback and CUE sheet handling
features.description: Natively handles CUE sheets for seamless, gapless playback of albums ripped as a single audio file.

features.title: Album art plugin
features.description: Fetches cover art from multiple local and online sources automatically.

features.title: Play from ZIP archives
features.description: Can play audio files directly from within ZIP archives without extracting them first.

features.title: On-screen notifications
features.description: An OSD Notify plugin displays track information on screen when a song changes.

features.title: Cross-platform builds
features.description: Available primarily for Linux and other Unix-like systems, with additional builds for Windows and macOS.

features.title: Customizable GTK3 interface
features.description: Later versions default to a GTK3-based interface that can be themed and rearranged to suit user preference.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers or licensing costs
Supports an unusually broad range of audio formats, including niche tracker and chiptune formats
Highly customizable through its plugin architecture, letting users tailor the feature set precisely
Lightweight and resource-efficient compared to many full-featured media players
Native CUE sheet support gives accurate gapless playback for single-file album rips
Actively used and packaged across most major Linux distributions
cons: Interface and setup can feel less polished or intuitive than mainstream commercial or cross-platform players
Plugin-based customization requires more manual configuration than an out-of-the-box all-in-one player
Windows and macOS builds receive less attention than the primary Linux target platform
As a volunteer-maintained open-source project, release cadence and support responsiveness can vary
Lacks the built-in streaming-service integrations that many modern commercial music players offer
Documentation for advanced plugin configuration is less comprehensive than for mainstream media players

faqs.question: Is DeaDBeeF free
faqs.answer: Yes, DeaDBeeF is completely free and open source with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What platforms does DeaDBeeF support
faqs.answer: It is primarily built for Linux and other Unix-like systems, with additional builds available for Windows and macOS.

faqs.question: What audio formats does DeaDBeeF play
faqs.answer: It natively supports MP3, FLAC, OGG, APE, WAV, WavPack, AAC, ALAC, Musepack, TTA, WMA, audio CDs, and tracker or chiptune formats like SID, NSF, MOD, and S3M, with even more formats available via plugins.

faqs.question: Why is DeaDBeeF plugin-based
faqs.answer: Its modular design lets users enable only the features and format decoders they actually need, keeping the core player lightweight while still allowing deep customization.

faqs.question: Does DeaDBeeF support gapless playback
faqs.answer: Yes, it has native CUE sheet handling that supports gapless playback for albums ripped as a single audio file.

faqs.question: When was DeaDBeeF first released
faqs.answer: It was first published in August 2009.

faqs.question: Can DeaDBeeF play music from ZIP files
faqs.answer: Yes, a plugin allows it to play audio files directly from ZIP archives without extracting them.

faqs.question: Is DeaDBeeF actively maintained in 2026
faqs.answer: Yes, it continues to receive incremental updates from its community maintainers, with recent versions adding features like FLAC network streaming and a lyrics plugin.

use_cases.title: Lightweight Linux music playback
use_cases.description: Provides a low-resource-usage audio player for Linux desktop users who want speed and stability over heavy features.

use_cases.title: Playing niche and legacy audio formats
use_cases.description: Lets retro-gaming and chiptune enthusiasts play tracker and console sound formats like MOD, NSF, and SID natively.

use_cases.title: Highly customized listening setup
use_cases.description: Allows power users to build a tailored player experience by selectively enabling only the plugins they need.
use_cases.audience: Advanced Linux users and audio hobbyists

use_cases.title: Managing large local music libraries
use_cases.description: Handles large personal music collections with CUE sheet support and broad format compatibility without needing format conversion.
use_cases.audience: Users with extensive local audio archives

enrichment.short_description: Dead Man's Snitch is a cron job and scheduled task monitoring service that watches for expected check-ins from periodic jobs, such as cron tasks, Heroku Scheduler runs, or any other recurring process, and alerts a team when a job fails to report in on time. Founded in 2012 and acquired by web development studio Collective Idea in 2013, it remains a widely used, straightforward monitoring tool for developers. It offers a free single-snitch plan plus paid monthly plans that scale up the number of monitored jobs and integrations.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription with a free single-job plan and paid monthly plans priced by number of monitored jobs (snitches)
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available for one job; paid plans start at 5 US dollars per month
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed; operated as a product of Collective Idea, a small web development studio
enrichment.seo_title: Dead Man's Snitch Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dead Man's Snitch monitors cron jobs and scheduled tasks, alerting you when they fail to run. See its pricing plans, features, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Dead Man's Snitch, at deadmanssnitch.com, is a cron job and scheduled task monitoring service built to solve a specific, common infrastructure problem: cron jobs and other periodic tasks tend to fail silently, and teams often only discover a missed backup, report, or sync job long after the fact. The tool works on a simple check-in model, sometimes called a dead man's switch pattern: a monitored job is configured to ping a unique URL, called a snitch, at the end of a successful run, and if that ping does not arrive within an expected time window, Dead Man's Snitch fires an alert to notify the team that something went wrong, whether the job crashed, hung, or never ran at all. This approach works for any periodic process, not just traditional Unix cron jobs, including Heroku Scheduler tasks, Windows scheduled tasks, CI/CD pipeline steps, and custom background workers, since the only requirement is the ability to make an outbound HTTP request on success. The service was founded in 2012 and was acquired by Collective Idea, a web development studio based in Holland, Michigan, on July 30, 2013, and has continued to operate as one of Collective Idea's products since. Dead Man's Snitch's positioning emphasizes simplicity: rather than being a full observability or application-performance-monitoring platform, it focuses narrowly on the question of whether a scheduled job checked in when expected, making it fast to set up and easy to reason about compared to heavier monitoring suites. Pricing follows a tiered structure based primarily on the number of snitches, meaning the number of distinct monitored jobs, a customer needs. The free Lone Snitch plan covers a single monitored job, suitable for hobbyists or very small setups. Paid plans include Little Birdy at 5 US dollars per month for 3 snitches with basic check-in intervals, API access, a Field Agent feature, and unlimited team members; Private Eye at 19 US dollars per month for 100 snitches with enhanced intervals and additional integrations; and Surveillance Van at 49 US dollars per month for 300 snitches with enhanced intervals, integrations, Smart Alerts, and Error Notices, which lets the service also flag jobs that check in but report an error status rather than simply going silent. All paid tiers include API access and unlimited team members, meaning pricing scales primarily with the number of monitored jobs rather than the number of people who need access, which is a notable difference from many other developer tools that charge per seat. Customers needing on-premise hosting, higher snitch limits, or more extensive monitoring capabilities beyond the published plans are directed to contact the company directly for a custom arrangement. Integrations typically include common alerting and chat channels such as email, Slack, and other webhook-based notification targets, letting teams route missed check-in alerts into their existing incident-response workflows. As a directory entry in 2026, Dead Man's Snitch is best understood as a lightweight, focused, and long-standing cron and scheduled-job monitoring tool, well suited to teams that want a simple, reliable way to catch silently failing background jobs without adopting a full observability platform, and its per-job rather than per-seat pricing model makes it especially cost-effective for larger teams monitoring a moderate number of jobs.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Dead Man's Snitch Works
paragraphs: Each monitored job is assigned a unique URL, called a snitch, that the job is configured to ping after it completes successfully. If the expected ping does not arrive within the configured time window, Dead Man's Snitch sends an alert.

This check-in model works for any process capable of making an outbound HTTP request on success, including traditional cron jobs, Heroku Scheduler tasks, CI/CD pipeline steps, and custom background workers, not just Unix cron specifically.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Pricing Scales
paragraphs: Pricing is based primarily on the number of snitches, or monitored jobs, rather than the number of team members, with a free plan for a single job and paid plans ranging from 5 to 49 US dollars per month covering 3 to 300 snitches.

All paid plans include API access and unlimited team members, making the pricing model notably cost-effective for larger teams that need many people to have visibility into a moderate number of monitored jobs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company Background
paragraphs: Dead Man's Snitch was founded in 2012 and acquired by Collective Idea, a web development studio based in Holland, Michigan, on July 30, 2013, and has operated as one of Collective Idea's products since the acquisition.

The service has maintained a consistent focus on simple, reliable job check-in monitoring rather than expanding into a broader observability platform, which has helped it remain a straightforward, well-regarded tool among developers over more than a decade of operation.

features.title: Simple check-in monitoring
features.description: Detects missed or failed scheduled jobs by watching for an expected HTTP ping after each successful run.

features.title: Works with any scheduler
features.description: Supports cron, Heroku Scheduler, CI/CD pipelines, and any custom process that can send an HTTP request.

features.title: Configurable check-in intervals
features.description: Lets teams define expected run frequency and grace periods per monitored job.

features.title: Error status detection
features.description: Smart Alerts and Error Notices on higher plans flag jobs that check in but report a failure rather than only detecting silence.

features.title: API access
features.description: Provides an API for programmatically creating and managing snitches, included on all paid plans.

features.title: Unlimited team members on paid plans
features.description: Scales pricing by number of monitored jobs rather than number of users.

features.title: Integrations
features.description: Routes alerts into common channels such as email and chat or webhook-based notification tools.

features.title: Field Agent feature
features.description: An additional monitoring capability included starting on the Little Birdy plan.

pros: Very simple, focused setup that solves the specific problem of silently failing scheduled jobs
Pricing scales by number of monitored jobs rather than by seat, which favors larger teams
Works with virtually any scheduler or periodic process capable of an outbound HTTP ping
Free plan is available for monitoring a single job at no cost
Higher tiers add error-status detection, not just silence detection, via Smart Alerts
Long operating history since 2012 under a stable, established web development studio
cons: Paid plan snitch limits (3, 100, or 300) may require jumping a tier as monitoring needs grow incrementally
Does not appear to publish a per-seat or usage-based enterprise pricing calculator online, requiring direct contact for custom needs
Company size and detailed internal metrics are not publicly disclosed
Focuses narrowly on check-in monitoring rather than broader application performance or infrastructure monitoring
Free plan is limited to a single monitored job, which is quite restrictive beyond personal projects
On-premise hosting is not offered as a standard published plan and requires a custom conversation with sales

faqs.question: What does Dead Man's Snitch do
faqs.answer: It monitors cron jobs and other scheduled tasks by watching for an expected check-in ping, alerting the team when a job fails to run or check in on time.

faqs.question: How much does Dead Man's Snitch cost
faqs.answer: There is a free plan for one monitored job, with paid plans starting at 5 US dollars per month for 3 jobs up to 49 US dollars per month for 300 jobs.

faqs.question: Does Dead Man's Snitch work with Heroku Scheduler
faqs.answer: Yes, it explicitly supports monitoring cron jobs, Heroku Scheduler tasks, and any other scheduled process capable of sending an HTTP check-in.

faqs.question: Who owns Dead Man's Snitch
faqs.answer: It was founded in 2012 and acquired by Collective Idea, a web development studio based in Holland, Michigan, in July 2013.

faqs.question: Does Dead Man's Snitch charge per team member
faqs.answer: No, paid plans include unlimited team members and instead scale pricing by the number of monitored jobs, called snitches.

faqs.question: Can Dead Man's Snitch detect a job that ran but failed
faqs.answer: Yes, on higher plans Smart Alerts and Error Notices can flag jobs that checked in but reported an error, not just jobs that went silent.

faqs.question: Is there an API for Dead Man's Snitch
faqs.answer: Yes, API access is included on all paid plans for programmatically managing snitches.

faqs.question: What integrations does Dead Man's Snitch support
faqs.answer: It integrates with common alerting channels including email and chat or webhook-based notification tools on its higher plans.

use_cases.title: Monitoring nightly backup jobs
use_cases.description: Alerts a team immediately if a scheduled database or file backup job fails to run or complete.

use_cases.title: Watching data pipeline and ETL jobs
use_cases.description: Confirms scheduled data syncs and ETL processes complete on time, catching silent failures before they cause downstream data gaps.

use_cases.title: Verifying scheduled report generation
use_cases.description: Ensures automated reports, invoices, or emails generated on a schedule actually run as expected.

use_cases.title: Tracking Heroku Scheduler tasks
use_cases.description: Monitors Heroku Scheduler jobs specifically, filling a gap since Heroku itself does not natively alert on missed scheduled runs.

enrichment.short_description: Dealpath is a real estate investment management and deal-pipeline software platform built for institutional commercial real estate firms to source, track, underwrite, and close acquisitions, dispositions, developments, and debt transactions in one centralized system. Launched in 2014, it is dual-headquartered in San Francisco and New York, with additional offices in Toronto and Austin. It sells custom-quoted Professional and Enterprise subscription plans requiring a minimum of five users, rather than publishing fixed self-serve pricing.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom-quoted enterprise SaaS subscription, sold via sales demo rather than self-serve signup
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing based on firm size and needs; a minimum of five users is required, with no minimum deal volume
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California and New York, New York, United States (with additional offices in Toronto and Austin)
enrichment.seo_title: Dealpath Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Use Cases
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dealpath is a deal-pipeline and investment management platform for commercial real estate firms. See its features, pricing model, and who it is built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Dealpath is a real estate investment management and deal-pipeline software platform built specifically for institutional commercial real estate firms, launched in 2014 and dual-headquartered in San Francisco and New York, with additional offices in Toronto and Austin. The platform centralizes the full lifecycle of a real estate deal, from initial sourcing and underwriting through due diligence, investment committee decisioning, and eventual closing, covering acquisitions, dispositions, development projects, and debt transactions within a single, shared system of record. Dealpath is positioned as the industry-leading deal management platform for real estate, and its customer base spans investment managers, developers, brokerages, lenders, and other institutional real estate organizations that need to coordinate deal activity across distributed teams. Core platform capabilities include AI-powered deal data ingestion, which extracts and structures data from offering memoranda, broker packages, and other unstructured deal documents; a comparables database with built-in analytics and reporting to support underwriting and valuation decisions; collaborative workflows purpose-built for due diligence, letting distributed teams track tasks, documents, and approvals against a single deal record; and pipeline management tools that systematize how deals move through stages toward an investment committee decision. The platform emphasizes Microsoft Office compatibility, letting teams that rely heavily on Excel-based underwriting models continue working in familiar formats while syncing data into the centralized platform, and it carries SOC 2 Type 2 enterprise security certification, a baseline expectation for institutional real estate and financial services buyers evaluating vendor risk. Dealpath sells two primary plans, Professional and Enterprise, both sold on a custom-quoted basis rather than published self-serve pricing; the company requires a minimum of five users per account but imposes no minimum deal volume, reflecting its focus on serving teams rather than solo users or very small shops. The Enterprise plan extends the Professional tier with additional capabilities aimed at larger, more complex organizations, including contextual deal data surfaced directly within investor reporting, business intelligence integration, the ability to leverage portfolio-level data across the organization for decision-making, integration with asset management platforms to connect deal-stage and post-close asset data, early access to exclusive product betas, single sign-on (SSO) support for enterprise identity management, an open API for centralizing Dealpath within a firm's broader technology ecosystem, and sandbox environments for testing configurations before rolling changes out broadly. Every plan level includes a dedicated Customer Success Manager, white-glove implementation typically taking six to eight weeks, ongoing end-user training and support, and an average support response time of about two hours, reflecting a high-touch enterprise sales and onboarding motion rather than a self-serve product-led growth model. Because pricing is not published, prospective customers must request a demo or contact Dealpath's sales team directly to receive a quote tailored to their firm's size, user count, and specific needs. As a directory entry in 2026, Dealpath should be understood as a category-leading, enterprise-focused deal management platform purpose-built for institutional commercial real estate investment workflows, differentiated by its combination of AI-assisted data ingestion, deep Microsoft Office compatibility, and a high-touch implementation and support model, rather than as a lightweight or self-serve tool aimed at individual investors or very small firms.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Dealpath Does
paragraphs: Dealpath centralizes the full commercial real estate deal lifecycle, from sourcing and underwriting through due diligence, investment committee decisioning, and closing, covering acquisitions, dispositions, development, and debt transactions in one system.

The platform is built specifically for institutional real estate organizations that need distributed teams working from a single, shared record of deal activity, rather than scattered spreadsheets, email threads, and disconnected documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Core Platform Capabilities
paragraphs: Key capabilities include AI-powered ingestion of unstructured deal documents, a comparables database with analytics and reporting, collaborative due-diligence workflows, and pipeline management tools that systematize how deals progress toward an investment committee decision.

Dealpath also emphasizes compatibility with Microsoft Office, letting Excel-centric underwriting workflows continue while syncing into the centralized platform, and it holds SOC 2 Type 2 certification for enterprise security compliance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Onboarding Model
paragraphs: Dealpath sells Professional and Enterprise plans on a custom-quoted basis, requiring a minimum of five users per account with no minimum deal volume, and prospective customers must request a demo to receive pricing.

All plans include a dedicated Customer Success Manager and white-glove implementation typically taking six to eight weeks, reflecting a high-touch enterprise onboarding process rather than instant self-serve signup.

features.title: AI-powered deal data ingestion
features.description: Extracts and structures data automatically from offering memoranda and other unstructured deal documents.

features.title: Comparables database
features.description: Provides a built-in database of comparable deals with analytics and reporting to support underwriting.

features.title: Due diligence workflows
features.description: Coordinates distributed teams around tasks, documents, and approvals tied to a single deal record.

features.title: Pipeline and investment committee management
features.description: Systematizes how deals move through stages toward a final investment decision.

features.title: Microsoft Office compatibility
features.description: Integrates with Excel-based underwriting workflows that many real estate teams already rely on.

features.title: Open API and BI integration
features.description: Enterprise plan customers can connect Dealpath to business intelligence tools and other systems via an open API.

features.title: Asset management platform integration
features.description: Connects deal-stage data with post-close asset management systems for continuity across the investment lifecycle.

features.title: SOC 2 Type 2 security
features.description: Meets enterprise security compliance standards expected by institutional real estate and financial services buyers.

pros: Purpose-built specifically for institutional commercial real estate deal workflows rather than generic project management
AI-powered data ingestion reduces manual data entry from offering memoranda and deal documents
Strong Microsoft Office compatibility fits existing Excel-centric underwriting workflows
SOC 2 Type 2 certification supports enterprise security and compliance requirements
High-touch onboarding includes a dedicated Customer Success Manager and white-glove implementation
No minimum deal volume required, only a minimum user count
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales demo and custom quote, adding friction for quick evaluation
Minimum of five users per account makes it impractical for very small teams or solo investors
Company size and detailed internal metrics are not publicly disclosed
White-glove implementation typically takes six to eight weeks, which is a longer onboarding cycle than self-serve tools
Enterprise-focused feature set may be more than smaller firms need, pushing them toward a higher-cost tier
As an enterprise sales-led product, it lacks a free trial or self-serve entry point for prospective users to test independently

faqs.question: What does Dealpath do
faqs.answer: Dealpath is a deal management platform that helps institutional commercial real estate firms source, track, underwrite, and close acquisitions, dispositions, development projects, and debt transactions in one system.

faqs.question: How much does Dealpath cost
faqs.answer: Dealpath does not publish fixed pricing; it sells Professional and Enterprise plans on a custom-quoted basis after a sales demo, with a minimum of five users required per account.

faqs.question: Who is Dealpath built for
faqs.answer: It is built for institutional real estate organizations such as investment managers, developers, brokerages, and lenders that need centralized deal tracking across distributed teams.

faqs.question: Does Dealpath integrate with Excel
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform emphasizes Microsoft Office compatibility so teams can continue Excel-based underwriting while syncing data into Dealpath.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Dealpath's Professional and Enterprise plans
faqs.answer: The Enterprise plan adds capabilities such as business intelligence integration, asset management platform integration, single sign-on, an open API, and sandbox environments on top of the Professional plan.

faqs.question: Where is Dealpath headquartered
faqs.answer: Dealpath is dual-headquartered in San Francisco, California and New York, New York, with additional offices in Toronto and Austin.

faqs.question: When was Dealpath founded
faqs.answer: Dealpath launched in 2014.

faqs.question: Does Dealpath offer implementation support
faqs.answer: Yes, all plans include a dedicated Customer Success Manager and white-glove implementation that typically takes six to eight weeks, plus ongoing training and support.

use_cases.title: Institutional acquisition pipeline tracking
use_cases.description: Centralizes sourcing and underwriting for real estate investment managers evaluating many potential acquisitions at once.

use_cases.title: Cross-team due diligence coordination
use_cases.description: Coordinates legal, finance, and asset management teams around a shared due-diligence checklist and document set for a single deal.
use_cases.audience: Real estate due diligence and legal teams

use_cases.title: Development project deal tracking
use_cases.description: Manages development-stage deal data alongside acquisitions and dispositions in the same centralized pipeline.

use_cases.title: Debt transaction and lender workflows
use_cases.description: Tracks debt transaction pipelines alongside equity deals for lenders and firms with mixed investment strategies.
use_cases.audience: Commercial real estate lenders and debt investors

features: Contextual deal data in investor reporting

enrichment.short_description: debugmail.io is a fake SMTP and email testing tool that lets developers capture, view, and debug outgoing emails from applications during development without those emails ever reaching real inboxes. It provides shareable team mailboxes, HTML and plain-text email previews, downloadable .EML files, and optional forwarding to real addresses for final review. The service offers a free tier, including a free plan for open-source projects, alongside paid Gold and Premium per-user plans for larger teams.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription billed per user per month, with a free plan (time-limited trial features) and paid Gold and Premium tiers
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start at approximately 2 US dollars per user per month
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed; operated by WBTech
enrichment.headquarters: Not publicly disclosed on the official site; third-party company databases list Moscow, Russia
enrichment.seo_title: debugmail.io Review 2026: Fake SMTP Testing Tool Pricing
